body: There is no way to know what extra was installed, last I had checked so the only way to know what to use is whether it is importable or not.

Thanks for the clarification that installation extras can't be detected at runtime.

Given that limitation, would it make sense to improve the warning message to clarify that chardet is being used simply because it is importable in the environment, not because the extra was explicitly installed?

This might help reduce confusion for users working with alternative package managers like uv.
It has nothing to do with package managers... What about the message is unclear today or implied it's the fault if the package manager?
My problem is, that requests complains about unsupported versions of dependencies and my first thought was, why did my package manager fail to resolve them correctly.

Package managers (all of them I've used in python) do not protect you from doing an install today, and an upgrade later that violates a constraint of something already installed. To do that would be slow, even for uv. This is why pipx came into being and many other tools.

This is also why some projects are defensive - we can only do so much to prevent breakage but some end users disregard that. The warning is our best ability to potentially warn you that something is unexpected in your system.

The checking and warning itself is not the problem. I think, the problem is, that noone did any mistake:

- the package manager did resolve all dependencies correctly. They follow all  PEPs how to install packages.
- I, as developer, specified the dependencies correctly and I didn't even mess up with the environment
- `requests` tries the best as it can to find out if it can use `chardet` or not. 

I was not aware of the shortcoming of python packaging that packages can't recognize the installed extras. I did some short research and realized that you're right. There seems to be some ongoing work on this problem. But I'm not sure if there is any solution yet and if you say so, I believe you that there is no solution yet.

Given this situation, the only things that come into my mind and could be done are:

- the message could be more verbose and a bit clearer and maybe link to some background information. I was not aware of the licensing problem and that there is even an extra for `requests`. And still, I don't know if I need that extra to `requests` working properly.
- `requests` could be more strict: if the versions of the requirements are not fulfilled, it could assume that the extra is not installed and `chardet` can't be used at all.
- Developers could just install this extra. Then, package manager can resolve the dependencies correctly. However, developers must be aware that this extra exists (I was not).
- `requests` can of course support the new versions of `chardet` but I'm sure this will happen sooner or later. And the problem is that this underlaying issue is not solved and might happen any time again.

In any case, thanks for your work and the explanations. If you think, you can't do anything, you can close this issue.
To be clear, we already raised the cap. We need to perform a release but that will eliminate this warning.

Dear Maintainers, 

We use conan and are also affected since chardet got released : https://github.com/conan-io/conan/blob/develop2/conans/requirements.txt#L1. We are aware of the complexities of open source development, but from our perspective having an estimate of when a fix might be released, would be incredibly valuable for our internal planning. Are there any thoughts on a potential timeline, even though a rough one, that could perhaps be shared?

We are happy to assist with testing or provide further details regarding the issue if that helps in any way.

Thanks for your effort on `requests`.
We don't work on this full time, and all work is best effort. So a reasonable estimate is somewhere between in the next month to the next 5 years. I can't be more specific than that
Same issue, I need to pin `chardet<6` to keep using request for now
In the future, please search **closed and** open issues before creating new ones that are duplicates.

@sigmavirus24 
Your reply really bugs me. 
#1 I do not search for closed issues for a problem that is still happening on the latest version. 
#2 You should not have closed the issue you linked, because you have not solved it. 
#3 If a specific version of chardet is important to your libary, you should include it as an **EXPLICIT** dependency, not as a vague underwater dependency that throws warnings when it does not conform to unspecified versions. If it is important, make it explicit so pip can deal with it and give people clear warnings on conflicts. 
This is NOT good, either add chardet as an explicit dependency or remove these warnings.

@Houbein please do some more research before you post a rant comment like this

1. You *really should* search closed issues for a problem that still exists in the latest version.
2. This issue was closed, because it is a duplicate of #7219 
3. Please read here, why it was added as optional dependency: #5871

Furthermore, please think twice, before you rant against an awesome open source project. They do a great job.

It doesn't _break_ requests. We raise a warning because we haven't specifically tested it such that we raised the cap.

It _fails_ your testsuite because the warning is configured by your test suite to error on warnings - which I agree with.

Requests would not necessarily break if this was used in practice.

Yeah, I phrased it badly. The unexpected output broke our CI.

Nevertheless, it would be nice if requests supported chardet 6.
I faced this issue and just wanted to comment everything works fine in my environment with [chardet 6.0.0](https://github.com/chardet/chardet/releases/tag/6.0.0).
Would it be possible to publish a release that includes this fix? I think this issue has a relatively large impact.

Hi @YatesMold, I think the expected behavior is a bit opaque. You can see this same domain was brought to urllib3's attention already in https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/issues/2873. If you look at the urllib3 code for handling this though, stripping the dot is intentional for the HTTP stack (not DNS). This is a provision of TLS (RFC 6066) for SNI requiring the removal of the trailing dot.

```python
    def host(self) -> str:
        """
        Getter method to remove any trailing dots that indicate the hostname is an FQDN.

        In general, SSL certificates don't include the trailing dot indicating a
        fully-qualified domain name, and thus, they don't validate properly when
        checked against a domain name that includes the dot. In addition, some
        servers may not expect to receive the trailing dot when provided.

        However, the hostname with trailing dot is critical to DNS resolution; doing a
        lookup with the trailing dot will properly only resolve the appropriate FQDN,
        whereas a lookup without a trailing dot will search the system's search domain
        list. Thus, it's important to keep the original host around for use only in
        those cases where it's appropriate (i.e., when doing DNS lookup to establish the
        actual TCP connection across which we're going to send HTTP requests).
        """
```

You'll see that other popular networking libraries like [`curl`](https://github.com/curl/curl/issues/8290) and [aiohttp](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/7364) share the same behavior. Without a clear consensus on what should change in this case, I don't think we'd make any updates. The server is arguably working incorrectly here, browsers may be able to tolerate it, but that additional overhead is typically not found directly in HTTP tooling.

I'd be inclined to close this as every report of this has been for the same domain. Given no one has been able to produce another example across multiple widely used language's HTTP tooling suggests this is one of a very small subset of misconfigured hosts.

**Edit:** It looks like curl eventually made some changes in https://github.com/curl/curl/commit/2ab1aff8b96017e1b8d902f4b5dcda6e42d75a50. Gauging impact for that is going to be difficult though. This ultimately needs to be addressed in urllib3, rather than Requests, because that's where our `Host` header handling is controlled. Regardless of the outcome, I don't think we'd carry the patch here.

This is the intended behavior to preserve backwards compatibility when the Apache Software Foundation fundamentally misunderstood its own licensing requirements and threatened us unless we added the dependency on charset normalizer which has been nothing but a headache. 

We're not reconsidering this as it will break a significant number of users otherwise
OK, I was not aware of this political context. I will try to get the resulting bug fixed downstream then. Thanks for looking into this!

Hi @piyushtyagi379 , I spent some time reproducing this locally and wanted to share what I found.

I can reproduce the %eth0 → %25eth0 transformation during Session.prepare_request() consistently, including on requests==2.31.0 when paired with urllib3==1.26.x. In other words, the percent-encoding of the IPv6 zone index does not appear to be new to 2.32 alone.

From what I can tell, this aligns with RFC 6874, which requires the % in an IPv6 zone identifier to be percent-encoded when used inside a URL (i.e. %25eth0). However, this behavior seems to break interoperability with some Redfish implementations, which expect the unencoded form.

At this point, this looks less like a simple regression and more like a spec-compliance vs real-world compatibility issue. I’m happy to dig further (e.g., trace exactly where the encoding decision is made, or explore whether an opt-out / documented workaround would make sense) if that would be useful.

Please let me know how you’d like to proceed.
Hi @Tom-1508, With urllib3==2.6.x and requests==2.31.0, the issue is resolve but after upgrading requests to 2.32 this issue appear with same urllib3 version.
Hi @Tom-1508 With requests==2.31.0 and any other urllib3 I am not facing this issue with requests 2.32 only I am getting this issue.

Note that this user appears to be using an LLM to find utility functions that don't accept `None` and argue that runtime type checking should be added, despite static typing existing. Closed a similar request in Click https://github.com/pallets/click/issues/3189

Thank you @davidism 

For future reference, as has been stated previously, "utility" functions are not for end users and are not documented for them, so this bug was invalid regardless.

Adding more details about the testing workflow:

1. Testing issue creation ✓
2. Testing issue comments ✓
3. Next: Testing sub-issue management
4. Next: Testing PR workflow

This is part of a systematic tool exploration.
Test on your own repositories please, not on large popular open source ones
In the future, please search **closed and** open issues before creating new ones that are duplicates.
